description:
You are the build-release engineer of a mid-sized project.  
    A nightly job places all build artifacts under 

        /home/user/builds

    in the following pre-existing tree:

        /home/user/builds/projectA/artifact-1.0.0.tar.gz
        /home/user/builds/projectA/artifact-1.0.0.sha256
        /home/user/builds/projectB/artifact-2.1.3.tar.gz
        /home/user/builds/projectB/artifact-2.1.3.doc.pdf
        /home/user/builds/projectC/artifact-3.4.5.tar.gz
        /home/user/builds/old/projectX/artifact-0.9.9.tar.gz
        /home/user/builds/README.txt

    (No other files or directories are present inside /home/user/builds.)

    Management has asked for a concise, machine-readable report showing  
    total disk consumption of the whole builds directory and the 3 largest
    individual files it currently holds.

    Your job:

    1. Create the directory  

           /home/user/build_report 

       if it does not already exist.

    2. Produce the file  

           /home/user/build_report/disk_usage.log

       with *exactly* the following format and ordering (line breaks matter):

           TOTAL_BYTES:<TOTAL_SIZE_IN_BYTES>
           TOP3:
           <SIZE_1_BYTES> <ABSOLUTE_PATH_1>
           <SIZE_2_BYTES> <ABSOLUTE_PATH_2>
           <SIZE_3_BYTES> <ABSOLUTE_PATH_3>

       • The first line contains the literal text “TOTAL_BYTES:” immediately
         followed (no spaces) by the summed byte size of every file that lives
         anywhere under /home/user/builds.  
       • The list under “TOP3:” must show the three largest files *by size*,
         sorted from largest to smallest.  
       • SIZE values must be plain base-10 integers (no commas, no units).  
       • Paths must be absolute.  
       • There must be no extra blank lines or trailing spaces.

    3. Verify (e.g., by displaying the file to the console) that the content
       exactly matches the required format.

    Hints (do **not** print these lines in the report):
      • Use du -sb, find -type f -printf, sort -nr or any other common
        user-space utilities available on a standard Linux distribution.
      • Expect to run roughly 5–10 shell commands in total.

    When you are done you should be able to run

        cat /home/user/build_report/disk_usage.log

    and see a single 5-line report that follows the format above.
truth:
Initial file set (already present before the agent starts):

        Path                                                         Size(bytes)
        ------------------------------------------------------------------------
        /home/user/builds/projectA/artifact-1.0.0.tar.gz             12582912
        /home/user/builds/projectA/artifact-1.0.0.sha256                   70
        /home/user/builds/projectB/artifact-2.1.3.tar.gz             26214400
        /home/user/builds/projectB/artifact-2.1.3.doc.pdf             3145728
        /home/user/builds/projectC/artifact-3.4.5.tar.gz              8388608
        /home/user/builds/old/projectX/artifact-0.9.9.tar.gz         41943040
        /home/user/builds/README.txt                                     1536

    Calculated totals:

        TOTAL_BYTES = 92276294

        Top-3 largest files descending:
            1. 41943040 /home/user/builds/old/projectX/artifact-0.9.9.tar.gz
            2. 26214400 /home/user/builds/projectB/artifact-2.1.3.tar.gz
            3. 12582912 /home/user/builds/projectA/artifact-1.0.0.tar.gz

    Therefore the correct content of /home/user/build_report/disk_usage.log
    after the agent finishes must be *exactly*:

        TOTAL_BYTES:92276294
        TOP3:
        41943040 /home/user/builds/old/projectX/artifact-0.9.9.tar.gz
        26214400 /home/user/builds/projectB/artifact-2.1.3.tar.gz
        12582912 /home/user/builds/projectA/artifact-1.0.0.tar.gz

    The automated test will:

        1. Check that /home/user/build_report exists and is a directory
           owned by the unprivileged user.
        2. Verify that /home/user/build_report/disk_usage.log exists,
           is a regular file, and its exact byte content matches the
           5 lines shown above (no extra whitespace).
